sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient is a 48-year-old male who has had a cough for approximately three months, initially noticing it after loading horses and experiencing a cold.
The cough has been intermittent and has resolved on its own at times.
The patient has a history of rheumatoid arthritis, currently controlled with methotrexate 7.5 mg weekly, and has a history of smoking, quitting 7 years ago.
The patient has a family history of breast cancer, but no lung cancer or other malignancies.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of rheumatoid arthritis, controlled with methotrexate 7.5 mg weekly, and a history of smoking, quitting 7 years ago.
The patient's mother has breast cancer, but is doing well.
Review of systems: The patient denies any pain, shortness of breath, or other symptoms.
Current medications: Methotrexate 7.5 mg weekly for rheumatoid arthritis.
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's vital signs are within normal limits: blood pressure 124/76, heart rate 70, respiration rate 16, oxygen saturation 98% on room air, and no fever.
Physical exam: The patient's lungs are clear and equal bilaterally on auscultation, with no clicks, rubs, or murmurs on cardiac exam.
The patient's extremities are pink, warm, and dry, with no edema, but with some swelling in the knuckles, which is consistent with rheumatoid arthritis.
Pulses are intact in all extremities, and capillary refill is brisk.
Test Results: The patient had a chest CT scan showing a solitary 2 cm nodule in the lateral aspect of the right upper lobe, described as smooth in appearance with no emphysematous disease.
Assessment / Problem List:

Assessment: The patient has an incidentally found right upper lobe lung nodule, which is a solitary 2 cm nodule in the lateral aspect of the right upper lobe, described as smooth in appearance with no emphysematous disease.
Problem list: Incidentally found right upper lobe lung nodule; Rheumatoid arthritis; History of smoking.
Plan:
I ordered a pulmonary function test (PFT) to establish a baseline for the patient's lung function.
I also scheduled a PET CT scan to determine if the nodule is metabolically active, which may suggest cancer or inflammation.

I plan to remove the nodule via video-assisted thoracoscopy, which involves making three small incisions on the side of the right chest and using a camera and scope to remove the nodule along with a small portion of the lung.
The patient will be under general anesthesia and the procedure will take approximately 1.5 hours.
The patient will be admitted to the hospital for one night and have a chest tube in place until the following day, which will be removed at bedside.
I also advised the patient to continue following up with their rheumatologist and continue their medication therapy as previously outlined.
Follow-up: The patient will be seen again in a few minutes to sign paperwork and begin the treatment process.
